access.accessMethod: Read the pinned CME submodule file in the repository.
access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for SSecr

rights.basis: The XML permits personal, research, and teaching copies when its statement is included, permits linking, and expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or non-profit content providers; it does not state a copyright owner or open license.
rights.component: Pinned 1997 electronic encoding and embedded availability statement
notes: These legacy item terms are not erased by the CME repository's software Unlicense or by rights in the medieval text and 1898 edition.

entities.description: The publication-bound repository file at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/SSecr.xml. It contains an outer Steele Note and three grouped TEXT children corresponding to the Royal, Lambeth, and Yonge versions.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for SSecr
notes: The file is 628,610 bytes and carries IDNO SSecr.
The CME submodule's Unlicense is software/repository evidence and is not treated as a blanket rights statement for encoded texts, editions, manuscript images, or provider metadata.

rights.basis: The medieval works and their known translators predate modern copyright by centuries; the texts were also published in Steele's 1898 edition, and the U.S. Copyright Office states that all works published before 1931 are now public domain in the United States.
rights.component: The medieval Middle English texts and their underlying pseudo-Aristotelian work traditions

rights.basis: U-M's current bulk-download page says CME raw files are available without restrictions on use or reuse, but the pinned SSecr file carries a stricter legacy item statement. The conflict prevents a broader copyright-status conclusion for this exact encoding.
rights.component: Current U-M CME raw-file distribution and digital presentation

entities.description: A 1997 electronic text keyed from Steele's 1898 edition, later completely proofed and corrected by Jean Borger and Kenneth Church and reviewed by Catherine E. Paul. Its editorial declaration includes print notes but excludes textual glosses, regularizes soft-hyphen line breaks, retains the second version's book divisions and the third version's dual milestone sets, and omits other front matter and the glossary.
entities.label: University of Michigan HTI electronic edition of SSecr
notes: The pinned file's legacy availability paragraph permits personal, research, and teaching copies with its statement and links, while forbidding redistribution by commercial or non-profit content providers.

The current U-M bulk-download description says CME raw files are available without restrictions on use or reuse. Because the item-specific embedded statement is stricter, both statements are retained instead of silently replacing one with the other.

rights.basis: U-M's current bulk-download page says CME raw files are available without restrictions on use or reuse, but the pinned SSecr file carries a stricter legacy item statement. The conflict prevents a broader copyright-status conclusion for this exact encoding.

bibliographic.citation: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum, ed. Robert Steele, with a glossary by T. Henderson, vol. I, EETS Extra Series 74 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1898).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 74
bibliographic.title: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum

entities.description: The immediate print source of the U-M encoding: Robert Steele's EETS edition of three Middle English prose versions, with T. Henderson's glossary. Steele printed the Royal, Lambeth, and Yonge versions on pp. 1–248, supplied manuscript lacunae from collateral witnesses, and printed the glossary on pp. 249–293.
entities.label: Steele, Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum (1898)
notes: The volume calls itself vol. I. No second published volume was verified.
The introduction and notes announced on the title page were postponed; the short Note explains the accelerated publication of Yonge's version.

rights.basis: The volume was first published in 1898; the U.S. Copyright Office states that all works published before 1931 are now public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Steele's 1898 edition, editorial text, and Henderson glossary

entities.description: A 30-folio parchment codex written in one neat mid-fifteenth-century hand. The shortened-French-derived Secrete of Secretes occupies f. 1r through f. 26r; a separate pseudo-Bernard epistle begins f. 26v.

holding.notes: Formerly belonged to John, Lord Lumley; the target is item 1.

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 18 A VII

notes: Both codex items are in the same neat hand.

entities.description: The common textual exemplar X reconstructed by Margaret Laing from shared omissions, errors, rare forms at the same running-text positions, and synchronized language changes in Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685. It is a transmission hypothesis, not the authorial original or an extant cataloged manuscript.
entities.label: Reconstructed proximate common exemplar of Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685
notes: Laing argues that disjunctive relict forms make direct copying of either extant manuscript from the other extremely unlikely.
No shelfmark, holding institution, date, or public reproduction is known for X.

entities.description: The principal witness for Steele's Governance of Lordschipes text. James describes a late-fifteenth-century parchment codex of one flyleaf plus 44 folios in a clear hand; f. 18 is mutilated, and the Secretum ends f. 42r. Steele supplied the lost f. 18 passage from Laud Misc. 685.
entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 501

notes: 32 lines to a page; modern morocco binding; f. 18 mutilated.

rights.basis: The fifteenth-century material and text are centuries outside the U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: Medieval manuscript material and target text

entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment codex whose first item is the same Middle English Secretum version as Lambeth 501, occupying ff. 1r–57v according to Laing. It independently descends from their proximate common exemplar and supplied Steele's text where Lambeth f. 18 is mutilated. The codex also contains Walter Hilton's Mixed Life, a medical treatise, and a South English Legendary extract.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 685

entities.description: The principal witness for Steele's James Yonge version. The target is the codex's second Middle English item, following an English Conquest of Ireland, and runs from f. 28v through f. 72r in Steele's milestone sequence. The current summary TEI assigns the parchment codex a 1400–1410 date that predates Yonge's securely dated 1422 translation; this unresolved conflict is not normalized away.

holding.notes: Formerly owned by Richard Rawlinson and bequeathed to the Bodleian in 1755.

entities.description: A complete late-fifteenth-century collateral copy of James Yonge's 1422 Secretum version in a parchment codex of 85 folios, ending f. 84r with a closing that differs from Steele's Rawlinson-based ending. Steele explicitly supplies printed p. 191 through p. 196 line 5 from Lambeth f. 46 line 21 while Rawlinson is defective. The XML's second L-marked folio sequence likely maps this witness but is not confirmed.

holding.notes: James describes the manuscript at printed pp. 780–781. No exact current CalmView record was found on 2026-07-15.

notes: 28 or varying lines to a page; late current hand; morocco binding with Carew shield inlaid.

entities.description: The conceptual anonymous French source-version tradition called F3 in the source scholarship. This entity does not stand for one identified surviving manuscript or assert that any extant F3 witness was the physical exemplar used by an English translator.
entities.label: French F3 version of the Secretum secretorum

entities.description: The conceptual Latin vulgate source tradition associated with Philip of Tripoli. It is modeled as one disputed alternative for the second English version, not as an identified physical exemplar.
entities.label: Latin vulgate version of the Secretum secretorum

entities.description: The conceptual French source version on which Steele says the greater portion of James Yonge's English adaptation depends. This entity neither identifies Yonge's physical exemplar nor treats his Irish additions and adaptation as translated wholesale from Jofroi.
entities.label: Jofroi of Waterford's French version of the Secretum secretorum

next_steps: Consult Manzalaoui's full 1977 introduction at p. xlvii through legitimate borrowing or library access.
Compare shared innovations in both English witnesses against representative Latin-vulgate and F3 witnesses before asserting encoded_from or transcribes relations to a source-language witness.
openQuestions.question: Does the Lambeth 501/Laud 685 English version descend directly from the Latin vulgate, as Steele states, or from Anglo-Norman French F3, as Manzalaoui argues?

next_steps: Compare every L-marked milestone against the complete MS 633 microfilm on reel 15 or the physical manuscript.
Request U-M production documentation for the dual milestone encoding.
openQuestions.question: Does the XML's L-marked folio sequence, approximately 3r–84r, represent Lambeth MS 633 throughout?

next_steps: Recheck British Library digitized content after cyber-attack recovery.
Ask Lambeth for complete microfilm-derived copies of MSS 501 and 633 and clarify reuse terms.
Use Bodleian mediated copying for Laud ff. 1r–57v and Rawlinson ff. 28v–72r, or request whole-codex scans if conservation and budget allow.
Do not label the two selected MS 633 LUNA images as a complete manuscript facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can complete reusable digital facsimiles of Royal 18 A VII, Lambeth 501, Laud Misc. 685, Rawl. B. 490, or Lambeth 633 be obtained?

next_steps: Consult the full Rawlinson Quarto Catalogue entry and physical codicology for part-level dating.
Ask Bodleian whether the summary date is a legacy whole-volume value requiring correction or qualification.
openQuestions.question: Why does the current Bodleian summary TEI date Rawl. B. 490 to 1400–1410 when its second item is James Yonge's securely dated 1422 translation?

next_steps: Ask Lambeth Palace Library to provide the current catalog URI and preservation-microfilm reference for MS 633.
Retain James's detailed entry and the exact LUNA records until a current holding record is supplied.
openQuestions.question: What is Lambeth MS 633's exact current CalmView record, microfilm identifier, and current request status?

next_steps: Inspect corrected copies, errata, and Steele's working references for a typographical or OCR-independent explanation.
Continue to identify the Yonge collateral witness as MS 633 based on Steele's 1898 supply note, James, LUNA, and World Microfilms.
openQuestions.question: What produced Steele's 1920 census reading 'Lamb. Carew 596' for the Yonge tradition, when Lambeth Carew 596 contains the Song of Dermot and the Earl and James catalogs Yonge's Secretum as MS 633?

next_steps: Request U-M HTI production history, raw-file version dates, and validation notes for SSecr.
Compare a current raw U-M download byte-for-byte and structurally with the pinned XML when an exact direct item download is exposed.
openQuestions.question: What exact production files and transformations connect Steele's print volume, the initial Sencor keyboarding, the complete Borger/Church correction, U-M's current presentation, and the pinned CME XML?

reviewNotes: Cataloging subject is the exact three-version electronic publication set, not one undifferentiated abstract Secretum secretorum.
Textual, physical, editorial, digital, and reproduction layers are kept separate; rights in one layer are not transferred to another.
The source cache was built in the canonical repository and hard-linked into the dedicated research worktree; final inode and checksum checks are required before integration.
Negative facsimile findings are dated interface searches, not claims that no private, onsite, or future photography exists.

lineage.summary: The checked-in SSecr XML is a pinned University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative encoding dated 1997 and keyed from Robert Steele's 1898 EETS Extra Series 74 edition. It preserves Steele's short Note and three distinct Middle English version streams while omitting other front matter and Henderson's glossary. Steele prints version one from British Library Royal MS 18 A VII; version two chiefly from Lambeth MS 501, supplying its mutilated f. 18 from Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 685; and James Yonge's 1422 version chiefly from Bodleian MS Rawl. B. 490, supplying a Rawlinson lacuna from Lambeth MS 633. Laing reconstructs a proximate common exemplar behind Lambeth 501 and Laud 685, so neither is modeled as copied from the other. The second version's direct source remains disputed between Steele's Latin-vulgate claim and Manzalaoui's French-F3 claim. The XML's L-marked second milestone sequence likely maps Lambeth 633 but remains unconfirmed. A complete public scan of the 1898 edition and compact supporting sources are cached. No complete free public facsimile was verified for any of the five physical manuscripts: Royal, Lambeth 501, Laud 685, and Rawlinson have none in the exact public interfaces checked; Lambeth 633 exposes only two selected LUNA images, of which f. 84r contains the work's ending and f. 85v contains only later pen trials. Physical, microfilm, and mediated-reproduction routes are recorded without treating them as public facsimiles.

catalogSummary.author: Pseudo-Aristotle (traditional attribution); anonymous translators of the first two versions; James Yonge (third version)
catalogSummary.date: c. 1400–1422; the first version is first securely attested in a manuscript dated 1430–1470
catalogSummary.region: The English Pale in Ireland for James Yonge's version; the first two versions' composition places remain unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum

catalogingSubject.label: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-bounded publication set represented by the pinned U-M/CME XML: Robert Steele's short introductory Note followed by three distinct Middle English prose versions of the pseudo-Aristotelian Secretum secretorum. The three versions are separate intellectual units with different translators, source traditions, witnesses, dates, and regional contexts. They are not collapsed into one generic medieval work, and Steele's 1898 edition, its manuscript witnesses, the U-M encoding, and the checked-in XML remain separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Robert Steele's Note
notes: This paratext is included because it is encoded, but it is not counted as a fourth medieval intellectual work.
contentParts.summary: A short editorial note defining the volume's three versions, identifying the first as French-derived and second as Latin-derived, characterizing the third as English of the Pale, and reporting late discovery of its extensive dependence on Jofroi of Waterford.

contentParts.label: The Secrete of Secretes (Royal 18 A VII version)

locators.value: f. 1r through f. 26r; the next work begins f. 26v

notes: The table retains chapters 55 and 56 but states that they were missing from Steele's manuscript copy; the body therefore does not supply them.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Middle English version rendered from a shortened French source and printed from British Library Royal MS 18 A VII.

contentParts.label: The Governance of Lordschipes (Lambeth 501 version)

locators.value: ff. 1r–42r; text begins after prefatory material and ends at 42a in James's recto/verso notation
Secretum text ff. 1r–57v; a corresponding passage supplies the mutilated Lambeth f. 18

notes: The table retains chapter 62 and chapters 70–75, but the source manuscript did not translate them and they are absent from print/XML.

Laing localizes both surviving scribal texts mainly to southwest Lincolnshire, with a short more southerly section and a later West-Midland component. That witness-level mixture is not projected as a composition place for the intellectual version.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Middle English version printed chiefly from Lambeth Palace Library MS 501, with a mutilated passage on Lambeth f. 18 supplied from Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 685. The two surviving copies independently descend from a proximate common exemplar and preserve layered scribal language.

contentParts.label: The Governaunce of Prynces or Pryvete of Pryveteis (James Yonge version)

locators.value: f. 28v through f. 72r in Steele's milestone sequence
full collateral copy, ending f. 84r; Steele supplies printed p. 191 through p. 196 line 5 from Lambeth f. 46 line 21 while Rawlinson is defective

notes: The XML retains a second folio-milestone sequence marked L, running approximately 3r–84r. Its editorial declaration says this likely represents Lambeth 633 but has not confirmed the identification.
Lambeth 633's ending differs from the Rawlinson-based printed closing; it is an independent collateral copy, not merely a fragment used to patch one passage.
contentParts.summary: James Yonge's 1422 Anglo-Irish translation and adaptation for James Butler, earl of Ormond, printed chiefly from Bodleian MS Rawl. B. 490 and supplied for one lacuna from Lambeth MS 633. Steele reports that the greater portion directly translates Jofroi of Waterford's French version.

dateStatements.display: 1430–1470 (date of British Library Royal MS 18 A VII)
notes: This dates the extant witness, not the otherwise undated act of translation.

Steele prints 'Soon after 1400'; the modern review of Ferster describes the version as written about 1400. The surviving Lambeth and Laud copies are fifteenth-century witnesses and do not themselves fix the translation date more narrowly.

extent.description: Three prose intellectual units plus Steele's editorial Note. The XML has 148 DIV1, 183 DIV2, 8 DIV3, 359 HEAD, 354 P, 9 L, 248 PB, and 379 MILESTONE elements. These are encoding metrics across uneven edition structures, not canonical chapter counts. The first printed table enumerates 62 entries but its chapters 55–56 are absent from the manuscript; the second table retains entries for chapter 62 and chapters 70–75 although those chapters were not translated in its manuscript and are absent from print/XML.

formStatements.description: Three prose versions plus Steele's prose Note. The XML's nine L elements include embedded verse and a two-line closing epigraph in the third version; they do not constitute a separate verse work or make the compilation mixed-form.

genres.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The immediate source family of the second version remains disputed; the classification does not settle whether it descends directly from Latin or French F3.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML PROFILEDESC lists Latin and French but omits the document's primary Middle English language. Direct text inspection confirms scoped embedded Latin in the second and third versions; no embedded-French statement is asserted without textual evidence.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: All 82 FOREIGN elements were inspected as Latin. This embedded material does not make Latin a co-primary language of the three-version publication set.

scope.description: One FOREIGN element occurs in the second version and 81 occur in the third; the third version also contains nine L elements, chiefly Latin verse and its closing epigraph.

notes: The first, second, and third version labels are editorially convenient only; each child part retains its own witnessed title and source history.
Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685 are sibling descendants of a reconstructed proximate common exemplar. Laud is also the supply witness for Lambeth's mutilated f. 18 in Steele's edition.

Lambeth 633 is a complete collateral copy of Yonge's version ending f. 84r, not merely the source of Steele's p. 191–196 patch. Its different closing and the unconfirmed L milestones are preserved as separate facts.
Steele's introductory Note says the second version has a Latin source. This remains a historical editorial claim, not a silently upgraded modern consensus.
The complete 1898 volume labels itself vol. I; no second volume was verified. The encoded selection omits the promised/postponed introduction and notes, other front matter, and Henderson's glossary.

next_steps: Consult Manzalaoui's full 1977 introduction at p. xlvii and later stemmatic work.

Compare shared innovations in Lambeth 501 and Laud 685 against Latin-vulgate and F3 witnesses before strengthening either tentative conceptual version_of relation or identifying a source-language witness.
openQuestions.question: Does the Lambeth 501/Laud 685 English version descend directly from the Latin vulgate, as Steele states, or from Anglo-Norman French F3, as Manzalaoui argues?

next_steps: Compare the complete Lambeth 633 microfilm or manuscript against each L-marked milestone.
Request U-M production notes explaining the retained dual milestone sequence.
openQuestions.question: Does the XML's second, L-marked folio-milestone sequence in James Yonge's version represent Lambeth MS 633 throughout?

next_steps: Consult Manzalaoui's full edition and later work on the F3 tradition.
Distinguish the Royal witness's 1430–1470 date and scribal language from the lost translation event.
openQuestions.question: Who made the first English version, when and where was it translated, and which exact French exemplar underlies its shortened text?

next_steps: Consult the full Quarto Catalogue description and obtain a codicological dating for the second item or hand specifically.
Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether the summary TEI date is a whole-volume legacy value requiring correction or part-level qualification.
openQuestions.question: How should the current Bodleian whole-codex date of 1400–1410 for Rawl. B. 490 be reconciled with the securely dated 1422 James Yonge translation preserved as its second item?

next_steps: Consult Manzalaoui's textual apparatus and later scholarship on Yonge and Jofroi.
Map Yonge's chapter sequence and Irish additions against Jofroi and the Latin traditions.
openQuestions.question: Which exact portions of Yonge's version derive from Jofroi of Waterford, and what sources underlie its remaining translations, adaptations, and Irish interpolations?

notes: Steele calls the work English of the Pale; Yonge addresses James Butler in his Irish lieutenancy and adds Irish historical and contemporary material. This statement does not transfer the later Rawlinson or Lambeth witnesses' holding places to the work's origin.
This is a conventional pseudo-author designation, not an attribution to the historical Aristotle accepted as genuine authorship.

scope.description: Traditional attribution inherited by all three English versions

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous translator of the first version
responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous translator of the first version

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous translator of the second version
responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous translator of the second version

scope.description: Adaptation of the Secretum tradition with Irish and contemporary political material; Steele identifies the greater portion as a direct translation of Jofroi of Waterford's French version

notes: T. Henderson's glossary belongs to the complete 1898 print volume but is outside the U-M/XML selection.

scope.description: The three-version edition and its encoded introductory Note

subjects.label: English colonial community and identity in medieval Ireland
Reigns of Henry IV, Henry V, and Henry VI
notes: The version is dedicated to James Butler in the context of Henry V's Irish lieutenancy and includes contemporary political material.

summaries.value: Three separately witnessed Middle English prose versions present pseudo-Aristotle's advice to Alexander on royal conduct, counsel, justice, health, diet, astrology, physiognomy, and practical wisdom. The first is an anonymous rendering from a shortened French source; the second anonymous version survives in closely related Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685 copies and has a disputed Latin-versus-French-F3 source path; the third is James Yonge's 1422 Anglo-Irish translation and adaptation for James Butler, earl of Ormond, largely based on Jofroi of Waterford's French version and augmented with Irish political material.

The cataloging unit follows the exact electronic publication set rather than inventing one homogeneous medieval work. Steele's encoded Note is paratext with zero medieval-work items. The three version parts retain separate manuscript and source bindings. The electronic selection includes the Note and printed text through p. 248, but omits the edition's other front matter and Henderson glossary; those print omissions do not erase the three-work boundary.

work.summary: Three separately witnessed Middle English prose versions present pseudo-Aristotle's advice to Alexander on royal conduct, counsel, justice, health, diet, astrology, physiognomy, and practical wisdom. The first is an anonymous rendering from a shortened French source; the second anonymous version survives in closely related Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685 copies and has a disputed Latin-versus-French-F3 source path; the third is James Yonge's 1422 Anglo-Irish translation and adaptation for James Butler, earl of Ormond, largely based on Jofroi of Waterford's French version and augmented with Irish political material.
